questions.question: According to research, what percentage of mentoring failures involve boundary violations or ethical breaches?

options.text: About 25% of failures involve boundary issues
About 50% of failures involve boundary issues
About 75% of failures involve boundary issues
About 94% of failures involve boundary violations or ethical breaches

questions.explanation: Research shows that 94% of mentoring failures involve boundary violations or ethical breaches—yet most informal wisdom-sharers lack formal ethics training. This makes boundary clarity and ethical awareness essential, not optional. Common violations include: overstepping competence, exploiting power dynamics, violating confidentiality, imposing values, creating dependency, and dual relationships.

questions.question: What is epistemic humility in the context of wisdom-sharing?

options.text: Never claiming to know anything or have any expertise
Recognizing the limits of your knowledge and that your experience is situated/partial, not universal
Only sharing wisdom if you have formal academic credentials
Assuming your experience applies to everyone equally

questions.explanation: Epistemic humility means recognizing that your knowledge is partial, situated, and shaped by your particular experiences and social location. You know some things deeply, but there's vastly more you don't know. It means naming your social location, qualifying claims ('In my experience...'), inviting adaptation, and acknowledging when something is outside your experience. It's the opposite of epistemic arrogance (assuming your perspective is universal).

questions.question: What should you do if a mentee shares suicidal thoughts during your conversation?

options.text: Keep it confidential and promise to save them yourself
Minimize it and change the subject to avoid discomfort
Take it seriously, assess immediate safety, connect to crisis resources (988), encourage professional help, and follow up—but don't become their therapist
Immediately end the mentoring relationship without explanation

questions.explanation: The ethical response is: (1) Take it seriously—don't minimize, (2) Assess immediate safety—are they in danger NOW?, (3) Connect to crisis resources (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline), (4) Encourage professional help (therapist, psychiatrist), (5) Follow up but DON'T become their therapist. What NOT to do: promise to 'save' them, take full responsibility for their safety, keep it secret, or provide therapy without training. Mentoring ≠ crisis intervention—know your limits.

questions.question: What is epistemic injustice, and why does it matter for wisdom-sharing?

options.text: When people lie or deliberately spread misinformation
When people are wrongly discredited as knowers due to prejudice, or when privileged knowledge is treated as universal while marginalized knowledge is dismissed
When someone doesn't have enough formal education
Epistemic injustice is not relevant to wisdom-sharing

questions.explanation: Philosopher Miranda Fricker identifies epistemic injustice as: when people are wrongly discredited as knowers because of prejudice, or when they lack conceptual resources to understand their own experience. In wisdom-sharing, privileged people's knowledge is often taken as universal truth, while marginalized people's knowledge is dismissed. Ethical wisdom-sharing acknowledges these dynamics and amplifies marginalized voices rather than centering your own privileged perspective as if it's universal.

questions.question: When should you recognize that a context is NOT yours to teach in?

options.text: Never—anyone can teach anything if they mean well
When dealing with clinical mental health issues, lived experiences you haven't had, contexts where you'd reinforce harm, or areas where you're personally unhealed
Only if you lack formal academic credentials in the topic
Only if someone explicitly tells you not to teach it

questions.explanation: You should step back from teaching when: (1) Clinical issues—someone needs professional care, not peer wisdom, (2) Lived experiences you haven't had—don't center your perspective on experiences that aren't yours (e.g., wealthy person teaching poverty survival), (3) Contexts where you'd reinforce harm—your teaching would perpetuate oppression or stereotypes, (4) Areas where you're unhealed—teaching from active wounds harms both you and learners. Good intentions aren't sufficient—some contexts require you to amplify others' voices rather than teaching yourself.

title: The Ethics and Responsibility of Wisdom Sharing - Quiz
